explanation: The candidate demonstrates a classic Stage 2 (Imperial/Instrumental Mind) orientation[cite: 1]. Their responses are primarily focused on self-interest, avoiding punishment, and maximizing personal reward[cite: 1]. For example, they view rules as 'obstacles you have to navigate so you don't get punished'[cite: 1] and state their main goal is to 'Look out for number one and protect their own interests'[cite: 1].

The candidate exhibits a clear Stage 3 (Socialized Mind) perspective[cite: 1]. Their identity and sense of self-worth are deeply embedded in their relationships and external validation[cite: 1]. They constantly seek to 'keep the peace'[cite: 1] and worry 'too much about what the other teachers think of me'[cite: 1], showing that their meaning-making is primarily driven by interpersonal alignment[cite: 1].

This is a solid Stage 3 (Socialized Mind) profile[cite: 1]. The candidate's responses revolve around fulfilling expectations and ensuring group harmony[cite: 1]. They state a desire to 'go with what everyone else wants so I don't cause a fuss'[cite: 1] and their greatest fear is 'letting someone down'[cite: 1], highlighting an externally defined sense of self[cite: 1].

The candidate shows a Stage 3 (Socialized Mind) orientation, heavily focused on professional and social perception[cite: 1]. They define success by having peers and superiors see them 'as someone who gets the job done perfectly'[cite: 1] and express a fear of making their team 'look bad'[cite: 1], demonstrating that their self-esteem is derived from the approval of their professional environment[cite: 1].

This profile reflects a Stage 3 (Socialized Mind) perspective situated within a military or highly cohesive group context[cite: 1]. The candidate's primary loyalty is to their peers, noting they are 'caught in the middle between pleasing my commanding officer and protecting my best friend'[cite: 1]. Their morality is defined by this 'brotherhood' and a fear of betraying the group[cite: 1].

The candidate demonstrates a clear Stage 4 (Self-Authoring Mind) level of development[cite: 1]. They possess an internal value system used to objectively evaluate external feedback[cite: 1]. They explicitly value the 'ability to think for myself and stand firmly by what I actually believe'[cite: 1] and emphasize acting 'in complete integrity with their own internal principles'[cite: 1].

This is a strong Stage 4 (Self-Authoring Mind) profile[cite: 1]. The candidate evaluates relationships and situations through a self-authored framework, striving to 'observe my own patterns, and choose my responses deliberately'[cite: 1]. They focus on 'living in integrity with their core values'[cite: 1] and taking responsibility for their own developmental journey[cite: 1].

The candidate exhibits a Stage 4 (Self-Authoring Mind) orientation with a strong focus on systemic design and personal accountability[cite: 1]. They view themselves as 'accountable for my choices'[cite: 1] and rely on evaluating complex situations 'objectively against my own standards'[cite: 1] rather than being swept up in interpersonal dynamics[cite: 1].

This profile is characteristic of a Stage 4 (Self-Authoring Mind) individual[cite: 1]. They emphasize maintaining 'my own distinct sense of self'[cite: 1] and defining worth 'by my own standards'[cite: 1]. They are capable of reassessing goals and adapting strategies independently when faced with obstacles[cite: 1].

The candidate demonstrates a Stage 4 (Self-Authoring Mind) framework, expressing strong internal leadership principles[cite: 1]. They rely on their 'own internal compass to make decisions'[cite: 1] and value the ability to make 'the hard right choices, even when they are unpopular'[cite: 1], showing independence from the need for external validation[cite: 1].

This candidate shows a Stage 5 (Self-Transforming Mind) level of cognitive complexity[cite: 1]. They embrace paradox and the limitations of their own frameworks[cite: 1]. They value the 'willingness to let my deeply held beliefs be dismantled'[cite: 1] and view identity as a 'constantly evolving system of beliefs, shaped by the friction of the relationships'[cite: 1].

The candidate exhibits a Stage 5 (Self-Transforming Mind) perspective, viewing relationships and identity as an ongoing process of 'mutual transformation'[cite: 1]. They recognize the fluidity of self, noting a 'willingness to let go of who I thought I was'[cite: 1], and they actively seek to 'dissolve boundaries' rather than rigidly defending them[cite: 1].

The candidate is in a 2/3 transitional stage[cite: 1]. While they still show a focus on concrete rules and duty, their orientation is heavily shifting toward a Stage 3 reliance on group loyalty and brotherhood[cite: 1]. They note taking failure personally due to the 'fear of letting down the people who are counting on you'[cite: 1], moving away from purely self-protective Imperial mindsets[cite: 1].

This candidate demonstrates a 3/4 transitional stage[cite: 1]. They are actively trying to establish a self-authored voice but remain highly reactive to external input[cite: 1]. This is evident in their tendency to get 'defensive when people try to tell me how to do my job'[cite: 1] and their conscious effort to 'not just follow the crowd'[cite: 1].

The candidate is in a classic 3/4 transition, actively fighting to differentiate their emerging internal voice from their interpersonal environment[cite: 1]. They state they 'butt heads a lot lately because I need her to understand I have my own worldview now'[cite: 1], showing the tension between Stage 3 relational embeddedness and Stage 4 self-authorship[cite: 1].

This profile shows a 3/4 transition characterized by a struggle between internal professional standards and the desire to maintain social harmony[cite: 1]. They have a self-authored perspective on what is right for students but experience 'dreading conflict so much that it makes me sick'[cite: 1], sometimes choosing to 'stay quiet... just to keep the principal happy'[cite: 1].

The candidate exhibits a 3/4 transitional stage, marked by a strong desire for autonomy but a lingering sensitivity to external control[cite: 1]. They are focused on asserting their independence and being 'the one making the final call'[cite: 1], yet they get easily 'defensive when corporate politics force me to compromise my vision'[cite: 1].

This candidate is navigating the 3/4 transition, moving away from seeking approval toward acting on internal principles[cite: 1]. They describe 'letting go of the need for everyone's approval when I have to make a tough, principled decision'[cite: 1], demonstrating the active construction of a Stage 4 internal compass amidst the remnants of Stage 3 socialization[cite: 1].

The candidate shows a 3/4 transition, aware of their own internal needs but still heavily bound by Stage 3 relational pressures[cite: 1]. They feel 'the pressure to always be nice and accommodating'[cite: 1] and struggle with 'not speaking up for myself when I know exactly what I should say'[cite: 1], highlighting the conflict between self-authorship and keeping the peace[cite: 1].

This profile illustrates the structural limit of Stage 3, forcing a transition to Stage 4[cite: 1]. The candidate is 'torn and exhausted from always being stuck in the middle' of conflicting external demands from work and family[cite: 1]. This crossfire of interpersonal expectations demonstrates the critical need for an internal, self-authored framework to resolve the competing demands[cite: 1].

The candidate is in a 4/5 transitional stage[cite: 1]. They have a solid self-authored foundation but are increasingly aware of the limitations of any single framework[cite: 1]. They demonstrate a 'growing capacity to hold multiple, sometimes contradictory truths'[cite: 1], yet sometimes catch themselves 'getting so caught up in seeing every side of an issue'[cite: 1], indicating the shift toward a Self-Transforming Mind[cite: 1].

This candidate exhibits a 4/5 transitional perspective[cite: 1]. They recognize that their 'most carefully constructed plans are just starting points'[cite: 1] and strive to hold their frameworks 'loosely'[cite: 1]. However, they acknowledge sometimes 'reverting to defending my watertight framework when I feel pressured'[cite: 1], showing the ongoing tension between Stage 4 certainty and Stage 5 fluidity[cite: 1].

The candidate is transitioning into Stage 5 (4/5 transition), finding liberation in deconstructing rigid theories[cite: 1]. They possess a 'newfound ability to comfortably dismantle the very ideas I spent twenty years fiercely defending'[cite: 1]. They are expanding their capacity to hold paradoxes but still recognize occasional slips into 'forcefully imposing my perspective on someone else'[cite: 1].
